title: The Localization Problem
concept: Localization is the process of predicting the coordinates of a bounding box surrounding a target object.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the primary output of an object detection model?
exercises.explain: Object detection models output class labels paired with bounding box coordinates.

cards.title: Where is the ball?
cards.body: In a sports broadcast, tracking the soccer ball is crucial. Knowing 'there is a ball' isn't helpful; the camera needs to follow it. This means the model must continuously predict the ball's location.

cards.title: What is Localization?
cards.body: Localization is the regression task of predicting the numeric spatial coordinates of a bounding box around a specific object in an image.

cards.title: Drawing a box vs. naming
cards.body: Think of a children's coloring book. Classification is like naming the animal: 'Elephant'. Localization is like tracing the dotted box around it so the child knows where to color.

cards.title: Face Unlock
cards.body: When you look at your phone, it localizes your face first to highlight it with a box before running facial recognition algorithms.

cards.title: Not just cropping
cards.body: Localization is not just cropping an image. It is predicting coordinates using regression, allowing the model to know exactly where the object lies without physically cutting the image.

cards.title: Classification vs. Localization
cards.body: Visualizing how localization adds coordinates to classification:

right.text:
Outputs category name AND coordinates.
e.g. "Car" at x=5, y=10

exercises.prompt: How is localization framed as a machine learning task?
exercises.explain: Localization predicts continuous numeric coordinates, which is a regression task.

options.text: As a classification task predicting category names
As a regression task predicting numeric coordinates

exercises.prompt: True or False: Localization and classification are often performed together by the same network in modern vision models.
exercises.explain: True. Modern networks use multiple output heads to predict both class probabilities and bounding box coordinates simultaneously.
Localization is the process of finding the position and boundary coordinates of an object.
exercises.sentence: Predicting the class is classification, while predicting the coordinates of the object is ___.

exercises.prompt: Which task represents pure localization without classification?
exercises.explain: Predicting only the coordinates (under the assumption of a fixed class) is pure localization.

options.text: Predicting the box coordinates for the main object in a dog image, assuming a dog is always present
Predicting if an image contains a dog, cat, or bird
Grouping similar images of dogs into clusters

exercises.prompt: You are building a system to scan barcodes on packages. The packages can be placed in any orientation. Why do you need localization here?

options.text: To classify the barcode into different product types
To find the coordinates of the barcode so the scanner knows where to read the lines
To crop the barcode and ignore the package entirely
To change the colors of the barcode for better lighting

exercises.explain: Exactly! The barcode must be located first (localization) so the scanning algorithm can read its details. Next: let's look at detecting one object vs. multiple objects!
